summary: Gruber addresses readers who think he's 'against' WordPress by clarifying his actual concern: self-hosted WordPress installations pose serious security risks if not kept constantly updated. He draws a parallel to the long-running Windows security debate, noting that WordPress's popularity may contribute to its targeting. He points out that competing platforms like Movable Type, Expression Engine, and hosted services haven't suffered comparable widespread attacks. While acknowledging WordPress's strengths and its improved upgrade process, he concludes that running your own WordPress installation demands constant vigilance as the price of admission.
key_points: Self-hosted WordPress installations face serious, recurring security exploits that other blogging platforms have largely avoided
The popularity-vs-poor-design debate around WordPress security mirrors the same unresolved argument about Windows that has persisted for 15 years
Blaming users for not keeping WordPress updated is a blame-the-victim response when other platforms don't require the same vigilance
WordPress has improved its upgrade process, but this doesn't help users already running older, compromised versions
Hosted services like WordPress.com, Squarespace, and Tumblr avoid these problems entirely by managing security for users
money_quotes: This isn't just a warning that the doors on your house have weak locks; this is coming home and finding your home burgled and your valuables missing.
The same argument continues to rage, 15 years after it started, regarding Microsoft Windows.
I can't abide the sort of blame-the-victim responses to these WordPress attacks along the lines of 'Well, what do you expect to happen if you don't keep your installation up to date?'
I don't religiously keep my installation of Movable Type up to date, and I know many other MT users don't either, and yet our sites don't get hacked.
I'm just saying the situation with WordPress is different, and clearly more dangerous, than it is on other platforms.
I fully acknowledge that there is much to be gained by running your own copy of WordPress. But clearly there is a price: constant vigilance.
key_insight: WordPress's security problems aren't simply a consequence of its popularity — the fact that comparable platforms don't suffer the same attacks suggests something deeper is wrong, and blaming users for not updating is inadequate.
